Project 1 — C02 Emissions vs. GDP per Capita (1990–2020)

Sang In Byeon

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE, fig.width = 7, fig.height = 4.5)
# install.packages(c("tidyverse","wbstats","janitor","skimr","broom","gt","GGally","readr","glue","yardstick"))
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
## ✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.2

## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()

library(wbstats)
library(janitor)
## 
## Attaching package: 'janitor'
## 
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     chisq.test, fisher.test
library(skimr)
library(broom)
library(gt)
library(GGally)
library(readr)
library(glue)
library(yardstick)
## 
## Attaching package: 'yardstick'
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:readr':
## 
##     spec
set.seed(301)

Intro and Problem

For Project 1, I wanted to see the relationship between CO₂ emissions per capita and GDP per capita across countries from 1990–2020.
Question: To what extent does a country’s income level influence its per-capita CO₂ emissions, and how effectively can statistical models capture and explain this relationship?

Loading the Data

I first try to load data from the World Bank using wbstats. If unavailable, I fall back to Our World in Data (OWID) and reattach World Bank region and income metadata so the rest is identical.

co2_code <- "EN.ATM.CO2E.PC"      # CO2 (metric tons per person)
gdp_code <- "NY.GDP.PCAP.KD"      # GDP per capita (constant 2015 USD)

fetch_worldbank <- function() {
  wb_data(
    indicator   = c(co2_pc = co2_code, gdp_pc = gdp_code),
    start_date  = 1990,
    end_date    = 2020,
    return_wide = TRUE
  ) %>%
    clean_names() %>%
    filter(!is.na(gdp_pc), !is.na(co2_pc)) %>%
    mutate(
      year    = as.integer(date),
      iso3c   = iso3c,
      country = country,
      region  = region,
      income  = income_level
    ) %>%
    select(iso3c, country, year, region, income, gdp_pc, co2_pc)
}

fetch_owid <- function() {
  owid <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/owid/co2-data/master/owid-co2-data.csv",
                   show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
    clean_names() %>%
    filter(!is.na(population), !is.na(gdp), !is.na(co2_per_capita)) %>%
    transmute(
      iso3c   = iso_code,
      country = country,
      year    = year,
      co2_pc  = co2_per_capita,
      gdp_pc  = gdp / population
    ) %>%
    filter(year >= 1990, year <= 2020)

  meta <- wb_countries() %>%
    transmute(iso3c, region = region, income = income_level)

  owid %>% left_join(meta, by = "iso3c") %>% drop_na(region, income)
}

dat <- tryCatch(fetch_worldbank(), error = function(e) NULL)

source_label <- if (is.null(dat)) {
  dat <<- fetch_owid()
  "OWID (fallback) + WB metadata"
} else {
  "World Bank (wbstats)"
}

glue("Data source used: {source_label}; rows: {nrow(dat)}")
## Data source used: OWID (fallback) + WB metadata; rows: 5050
dat <- dat %>% clean_names()
dim(dat)
## [1] 5050    7
head(dat) %>% gt()
iso3c country year co2_pc gdp_pc region income
AFG Afghanistan 1990 0.168 1084.7043 Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan Low income
AFG Afghanistan 1991 0.156 984.3518 Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan Low income
AFG Afghanistan 1992 0.112 954.7072 Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan Low income
AFG Afghanistan 1993 0.100 658.1321 Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan Low income
AFG Afghanistan 1994 0.089 487.3518 Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan Low income
AFG Afghanistan 1995 0.083 721.1792 Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan Low income

skim(dat %>% select(gdp_pc, co2_pc))
Data summary

Variable type: numeric

skim_variable n_missing complete_rate mean sd p0 p25 p50 p75 p100 hist
gdp_pc 0 1 13889.13 16041.75 361.19 2762.56 8034.56 19324.86 163531.58 ▇▁▁▁▁

Pre-Processing the Data

I cleaned up the dataset by standardizing column names and removing any missing values. Then I added a log-transformed version of GDP per capita to make the relationship more linear for modeling. Finally, I split the data into 80% for training and 20% for testing to evaluate how well the model performs on new data.

dat_model <- dat %>% mutate(log_gdp_pc = log1p(gdp_pc))

set.seed(301)
idx   <- sample(seq_len(nrow(dat_model)), size = 0.8*nrow(dat_model))
train <- dat_model[idx, ]
test  <- dat_model[-idx, ]

nrow(train); nrow(test)
## [1] 4040
## [1] 1010

Data Exploration

Overall Relationship (Scatter with LOESS)

train %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_pc, y = co2_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  labs(x = "GDP per capita", y = "CO₂ per capita (t/person)",
       title = "CO₂ vs GDP per capita (Train, 1990–2020)") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE)

Observation. Emissions per person rise with income, with diminishing returns at high income levels.

Facet by Income Group

train %>%
  mutate(income = forcats::fct_relevel(income, "Low income","Lower middle income","Upper middle income","High income")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(gdp_pc, co2_pc, color = income)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, size = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  labs(x = "GDP per capita", y = "CO₂ per capita", color = "Income group",
       title = "Relationship differs by income category") +
  facet_wrap(~ income, scales = "free")

Building the Predictive Model

I start with a simple linear regression using only log(GDP per capita).

m1 <- lm(co2_pc ~ log_gdp_pc, data = train)
summary(m1) %>% broom::tidy()
## # A tibble: 2 × 5
##   term        estimate std.error statistic p.value
##   <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>
## 1 (Intercept)   -27.5     0.590      -46.6       0
## 2 log_gdp_pc      3.64    0.0656      55.4       0

Plotting the Training Model

train %>%
  mutate(.fitted = predict(m1, newdata = train)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(log_gdp_pc, co2_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_line(aes(y = .fitted), color = "steelblue") +
  labs(x = "log(GDP per capita + 1)", y = "CO₂ per capita",
       title = "Model 1: Fitted line on training data")

Plotting the Testing Model

test %>%
  mutate(.fitted = predict(m1, newdata = test)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(log_gdp_pc, co2_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_line(aes(y = .fitted), color = "steelblue") +
  labs(x = "log(GDP per capita + 1)", y = "CO₂ per capita",
       title = "Model 1: Predictions on test data")

Basic Model Summary

pred_m1 <- augment(m1, newdata = test)
tibble(
  model = "Linear (log GDPpc)",
  rmse  = yardstick::rmse_vec(truth = test$co2_pc, estimate = pred_m1$.fitted),
  mae   = yardstick::mae_vec (truth = test$co2_pc, estimate = pred_m1$.fitted),
  r2    = yardstick::rsq_vec (truth = test$co2_pc, estimate = pred_m1$.fitted)
) %>% gt() %>% fmt_number(decimals = 3)
model rmse mae r2
Linear (log GDPpc) 12.625 3.534 0.098

Plotting the Residuals

par(mfrow = c(1,2))
plot(m1, which = 1)  # residuals vs fitted
plot(m1, which = 2)  # QQ plot

par(mfrow = c(1,1))

Improving the Model

I add income group and region as categorical predictors to capture structural differences.

m2 <- lm(co2_pc ~ log_gdp_pc + income + region, data = train)
summary(m2) %>% broom::tidy()
## # A tibble: 12 × 5
##    term                                    estimate std.error statistic  p.value
##    <chr>                                      <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>
##  1 (Intercept)                              -12.6       1.38     -9.10  1.42e-19
##  2 log_gdp_pc                                 2.29      0.133    17.2   4.08e-64
##  3 incomeLow income                          -4.46      0.471    -9.47  4.76e-21
##  4 incomeLower middle income                 -5.75      0.346   -16.6   6.07e-60
##  5 incomeNot classified                      -2.72      0.687    -3.96  7.76e- 5
##  6 incomeUpper middle income                 -3.75      0.240   -15.7   9.75e-54
##  7 regionEurope & Central Asia               -1.16      0.264    -4.41  1.05e- 5
##  8 regionLatin America & Caribbean           -1.98      0.293    -6.75  1.69e-11
##  9 regionMiddle East, North Africa, Afgha…    4.02      0.295    13.6   2.06e-41
## 10 regionNorth America                        6.03      0.680     8.87  1.09e-18
## 11 regionSouth Asia                           0.354     0.529     0.670 5.03e- 1
## 12 regionSub-Saharan Africa                   0.594     0.286     2.08  3.76e- 2

Building the Improved Predictive Model

pred_m2 <- augment(m2, newdata = test)

bind_rows(
  tibble(model = "Linear (log GDPpc)",
         rmse  = yardstick::rmse_vec(truth = test$co2_pc, estimate = pred_m1$.fitted),
         mae   = yardstick::mae_vec (truth = test$co2_pc, estimate = pred_m1$.fitted),
         r2    = yardstick::rsq_vec (truth = test$co2_pc, estimate = pred_m1$.fitted)),
  tibble(model = "Multiple (+ income + region)",
         rmse  = yardstick::rmse_vec(truth = test$co2_pc, estimate = pred_m2$.fitted),
         mae   = yardstick::mae_vec (truth = test$co2_pc, estimate = pred_m2$.fitted),
         r2    = yardstick::rsq_vec (truth = test$co2_pc, estimate = pred_m2$.fitted))
) %>% gt() %>% fmt_number(decimals = 3)
model rmse mae r2
Linear (log GDPpc) 12.625 3.534 0.098
Multiple (+ income + region) 12.172 3.094 0.162

Plotting the Improved Training Model

train %>%
  mutate(.fitted = predict(m2, newdata = train)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(log_gdp_pc, co2_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_line(aes(y = .fitted), color = "darkorange") +
  labs(x = "log(GDP per capita + 1)", y = "CO₂ per capita",
       title = "Model 2: Fitted values on training data")

Plotting the Improved Testing Model

test %>%
  mutate(.fitted = predict(m2, newdata = test)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(log_gdp_pc, co2_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_line(aes(y = .fitted), color = "darkorange") +
  labs(x = "log(GDP per capita + 1)", y = "CO₂ per capita",
       title = "Model 2: Predictions on test data")

Improved Model Summary

par(mfrow = c(1,2))
plot(m2, which = 1)
plot(m2, which = 2)

par(mfrow = c(1,1))

Specific Comparisons

test %>%
  mutate(.pred_m1 = predict(m1, newdata = test),
         .pred_m2 = predict(m2, newdata = test)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(.pred_m1, .pred_m2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_abline(linetype = 2) +
  labs(x = "Predicted CO₂ (Model 1)", y = "Predicted CO₂ (Model 2)",
       title = "Comparing Predictions: Simple vs Multiple Models")

Conclusion

  • Income level accounts for a significant portion of variation in CO₂ per capita.
  • Incorporating income group and region improved the model’s accuracy, highlighting key structural and regional factors.
  • This indicates that effective policy and energy systems help decouple economic growth from rising emissions in some regions.

Dataset Citation

World Bank Open Data (accessed via wbstats) and Our World in Data.
If the World Bank API was unavailable during knit, the analysis used OWID and World Bank metadata for region and income.
